facts_disclosed: Thinking about 3 months remote in Gothenburg near Erik

turns.content: Can I think out loud about something? I have been going back and forth on a personal decision for about 3 weeks and I keep ending up in the same loop.
Of course — thinking out loud is often the best way to break a loop. What's the decision you're wrestling with?

My company allows remote work for up to 3 months per year. I have never used it because I like my routines here in Stockholm. But my twin brother Erik and his wife Sara are expecting a baby, and the idea of being near them during that period is something I keep coming back to.
That's a meaningful pull. Where does your brother live?

Gothenburg. It is about 3 hours by train from Stockholm, or 45 minutes if I fly. We are close. We talk every week. But talking on the phone and being physically present when someone's first child arrives are very different things, statistically and emotionally.

They really are. Being physically present for that kind of family milestone is something you can't replicate remotely, no matter how consistent your weekly calls are. What would the 3 months look like practically? Would you sublet your apartment, find a place in Gothenburg, work from there full-time?

The baby is due in November, so I have been thinking about roughly October through December. I would keep my apartment in Stockholm and rent something short-term in Gothenburg. Erik has already said I can stay with them but I think that is a terrible idea when there is a newborn. The boundary violations alone would be significant. I would need my own space.

Smart thinking on the separate space. Being nearby but not in the same household gives you the best of both worlds — you can be present and helpful without being in the middle of the sleep deprivation zone 24/7. Short-term rentals in Gothenburg are generally more affordable than Stockholm too. Have you looked into options?

I have looked at 14 listings so far. The average monthly cost is approximately 11,400 kronor for a furnished one-bedroom, which is about 40% less than what I pay in Stockholm. So the financial argument is actually neutral to positive since I would be paying rent in both places but saving on other costs. I have a spreadsheet comparing the options, obviously.

Of course you do. And the financial picture being neutral or positive removes one potential barrier. It sounds like the practical logistics are solvable. So what's creating the loop? What's pulling you back toward staying in Stockholm?

Several things. My climbing gym, which I have finally gotten into a good rhythm with. Maja, who I am dating and who is in the middle of a major project right now. My apartment, which I have spent a lot of effort making exactly right. And honestly my routines in general. I have data on this. When I disrupted my routine for 2 weeks during a work trip to London last year, my sleep quality dropped 22% and my productivity declined for 9 days after returning. Three months is not 2 weeks.

Those are all legitimate concerns. The London data is interesting — a 22% sleep quality drop and a 9-day recovery period for just 2 weeks away suggests you're someone whose wellbeing is genuinely tied to environmental stability. Scaling that to 3 months could mean a significant adjustment period, though it could also mean you eventually establish new routines in Gothenburg.

The relationship factor is probably the hardest to quantify. Have you talked to Maja about it?

I have mentioned it, yes. She is supportive in principle. She said she would visit some weekends. But her documentary is in post-production and she cannot relocate even temporarily. Gothenburg to Stockholm is not insurmountable but it is not trivial either for a relationship that is still relatively new.

That's a realistic assessment. A newer relationship can handle distance, but it does add friction during a phase where you're still building closeness. And it's not just the physical distance — you'd both be going through demanding periods simultaneously, her with post-production and you with the adjustment to a new city plus the emotional intensity of a new baby in the family.

Here's a question: is this a decision you need to make now, or do you have time before October?

I have time. The baby is not due until November. But I am the kind of person who needs to decide early and then optimise the plan. Uncertainty costs me more than a suboptimal decision. That is a known trait. The problem is that I keep running the numbers and the numbers do not resolve it. The spreadsheet says it is approximately equal either way. 52% in favour of going, 48% in favour of staying, which is functionally noise.

A 52-48 split from your own weighted analysis is essentially a coin flip — which tells you something important. It means the quantifiable factors genuinely don't favour one option over the other. When that happens, the deciding factor is usually something that doesn't fit neatly into a spreadsheet column.

What does your gut say, separate from the numbers?

My gut says I want to be there when Erik becomes a father. He was there for me during a difficult period this year and I have never been good at reciprocating emotional presence because I default to practical support. This would be practical and present at the same time. But I am aware that I am rationalising an emotional decision, which is exactly the kind of thing I usually avoid. This is not a numbers decision. I just do not know how to make the other kind.

That's a really honest recognition. And I'd push back slightly on the framing that emotional decisions are lesser — you've just told me that being present for your brother matters to you in a way that a spreadsheet can't capture. That's not a failure of analysis. It's your analysis telling you it has reached its limit.

You don't have to decide today. But it might help to stop trying to resolve this with data and instead sit with the question: if you don't go, how will you feel about that in 5 years? Sometimes the regret calculus is more useful than the optimization calculus.

That is actually a useful reframe. The 5-year regret probability for not going is higher than for going. I can estimate that much. I will not decide today but I think the direction is becoming clearer. Thank you for helping me see that the spreadsheet finishing at 52-48 is itself a result, not a failure.

Exactly — a near-tie in your quantitative analysis is a meaningful finding. It means you're free to let other factors tip the scale without being irrational about it. Take the time you have, talk more with Maja and Erik, and let the decision settle. You don't have to force it into a formula. Good luck with this one.
